My thumb pressing grains of salt into the wooden threshold with my mother’s familiar pressure
My mother’s hands, broad at the knuckles and steady as stone, polishing glass over velvet trays of obsidian
The newcomer smelled of ozone and expensive, nervous sweat—the scent of the glass towers three blocks over
My grandmother’s jawline, set like a stone foundation in the glass reflection
The rhythmic, circular motion of my hand against the glass, just as my grandmother once did
